title: Crypto Swap Fee Breakdown: What You Really Pay For

friendlyUrls.anchor: Quote details can expire before sending

keyQuestions: What costs can be included in a crypto swap fee breakdown?
Why can the final crypto swap output differ from a market chart price?
How does the source wallet network fee affect a swap?
How do fixed-rate and floating-rate swaps affect fee expectations?
Why can confirmation time change the real cost of a crypto swap?
How should users compare crypto swap costs before sending funds?
quickSteps: Enter the exact source amount and pair before judging the route cost.
Check the source wallet network fee separately from the swap quote.
Compare the estimated destination output, not only the visible fee label.
Match the fee expectation to the rate model, fixed or floating.
Send only while the quote, amount, network, and destination details still match.

issueRouting.start: User wants to understand the real cost of a crypto swap before sending funds.

branches.if: The user only checks the visible fee label
then: Compare the estimated destination output instead of one fee line.
Check whether source wallet fees are separate from the route quote.
Review liquidity spread, payout cost, and rate model before sending.

branches.if: The source network fee is high or confirmations may be slow
then: Check the wallet network fee before broadcasting the transaction.
Compare the fee choice with the quote timing.
Use a fresh quote if the route conditions or timer may have changed.

branches.if: The user is choosing between fixed-rate and floating-rate swaps
then: Use fixed-rate logic when output certainty matters more.
Use floating-rate logic only when live movement is acceptable.
Send only when the amount, network, quote type, and destination details match.

riskNotes: A crypto swap fee can include source wallet cost, route pricing, liquidity spread, payout cost, and timing effects.
A market chart price does not include the full wallet-to-wallet execution path.
Low source network fees can increase timing uncertainty if the transaction confirms too slowly.
A quote that has expired may no longer describe the active route cost or expected output.
Splitting a swap into too many parts can multiply source fees, route checks, and payout costs.
